title: GMAT Data Insights: How to Master the Newest Section of the Exam
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The Data Insights section is the newest addition to the GMAT Focus Edition, and for many test-takers, it is the most unfamiliar. It tests your ability to interpret data presented in multiple formats, to reason across different sources of information, and to draw conclusions from quantitative evidence. These are precisely the skills that business schools and employers value, and a strong performance in this section can distinguish your application. Here is how the section works and how to prepare for it.

What Data Insights Actually Tests

The Data Insights section contains twenty questions to be answered in forty-five minutes. The questions are drawn from five types. Data Sufficiency questions test your ability to determine whether given information is sufficient to answer a question. Multi-Source Reasoning questions present information across two or three tabs, such as a written passage, a table, and a graph, and ask you to synthesize information across these sources. Table Analysis questions present a sortable table of data and ask you to evaluate statements based on the information. Graphics Interpretation questions present a chart or graph and ask you to complete statements by selecting the correct values from drop-down menus. Two-Part Analysis questions present a scenario and ask you to select answers for two related components.

The common thread across all five question types is the need to extract relevant information efficiently and reason with it accurately. The Data Insights section is not a math test, although it involves numbers. It is a test of your ability to work with information in the forms that business professionals encounter daily. A manager reviewing a financial report, a consultant analyzing market data, or an entrepreneur evaluating a business plan all use the skills that Data Insights tests.

How to Approach Each Question Type

Data Sufficiency: These questions appear in both the Quantitative and Data Insights sections. The approach is the same. Evaluate each statement independently. Determine whether it provides enough information to answer the question uniquely. Do not calculate the answer unless necessary. The sufficiency of the information is what matters, not the answer itself.

Multi-Source Reasoning: These questions are the most time-intensive. Start by reading the question first. Knowing what you are being asked helps you focus on the relevant information across the multiple tabs. Do not try to absorb all the information before looking at the questions. Navigate the tabs strategically. Some questions require information from only one tab. Others require you to combine information from multiple tabs. Identify which tabs are relevant for each question before you search for the answer.

Table Analysis: The table provided is sortable by column. Use the sorting function. It is there for a reason. Sorting the table by the relevant column can make patterns immediately visible and turn a complex question into a simple one. The questions typically ask you to evaluate whether statements are true or false based on the data. Check each statement against the table. Do not rely on your visual impression of the data. Verify with the actual numbers.

Graphics Interpretation: These questions present a chart or graph with missing information in accompanying statements. Your task is to select the correct values from drop-down menus to complete the statements accurately. Read the graph carefully. Note the axes, the units, the scale, and any labels. A common error is misreading the scale, particularly when the graph uses logarithmic scales or truncated axes. The GMAT often includes graphs where a visual impression is misleading. Trust the numbers, not your eyes.

Two-Part Analysis: These questions present a scenario and ask you to select answers for two related components. The two parts are often linked. The answer to the first part may constrain the possible answers for the second. Solve systematically. Consider each component in turn, but keep the relationship between them in mind. Plugging in values and testing them against the conditions is often more efficient than solving algebraically.

Building Data Interpretation Skills

Data Insights performance improves with practice, but the practice must be specific. Work through the Data Insights questions in the GMAT Official Guide. These are real, retired questions that accurately reflect the difficulty and style of the actual exam. Third-party questions vary in quality and may not replicate the GMAT's distinctive approach to data interpretation.

Develop the habit of reading charts and graphs critically in your daily life. When you encounter a graph in a news article or a business report, ask yourself what the graph actually shows. What are the axes? What is the scale? Is the visual impression consistent with the numbers? Does the conclusion drawn in the headline match the data? The critical reading of data is a skill that develops over time with consistent practice.

Practice working with multiple sources of information. The Multi-Source Reasoning questions are challenging because they require you to hold information from different tabs in your working memory. Practice by reading business case studies, financial reports, or analytical articles that combine text, tables, and charts. As you read, ask yourself how the different pieces of information relate to each other. What does the text explain that the table shows? What does the graph reveal that the text only mentions? The integration of information across formats is the core skill that Multi-Source Reasoning tests.

Timing Strategy for Data Insights

With twenty questions in forty-five minutes, you have roughly two minutes and fifteen seconds per question on average. Multi-Source Reasoning questions typically take longer. Data Sufficiency and Graphics Interpretation questions can be answered more quickly. Use the faster question types to bank time for the more demanding ones.

If you encounter a Multi-Source Reasoning set that is taking too long, do not abandon it entirely, but do not let it consume your section. Answer the questions as well as you can using the information you have gathered and move on. The time you save can be invested in questions you are more likely to answer correctly.

The Bottom Line

The GMAT Data Insights section tests your ability to interpret and reason with data in multiple formats. Master each question type with specific strategies. Use the sorting function in Table Analysis. Verify visual impressions against actual numbers in Graphics Interpretation. Read questions before diving into Multi-Source Reasoning passages. Build your data interpretation skills through deliberate practice with official GMAT questions and critical reading of real-world data. Manage your time by using faster question types to create space for more demanding ones. Data Insights is unfamiliar to most test-takers, which makes it an opportunity. Strong preparation in this section can give you an edge.
